Lex Fridman: Jensen, thank you so much for joining me today. I want to start with a question that I think a lot of people are curious about. When you look at the current trajectory of artificial intelligence and the role that NVIDIA has played in making this revolution possible, what surprises you the most about where we are right now compared to where you thought we would be ten years ago? Jensen Huang: Thank you Lex, it is great to be here. You know, ten years ago we were just beginning to see the early signs that deep learning could be something transformational. We had AlexNet, we had some early demonstrations, but I do not think anyone could have predicted just how quickly this would scale. The thing that surprises me the most is the universality of it. We thought GPUs would be great for training neural networks, and they are, but we did not anticipate that the same architecture would be useful for protein folding, for drug discovery, for climate modeling, for robotics, for autonomous vehicles. The breadth of application is what genuinely took me by surprise. Lex Fridman: That is a fascinating point about universality. When you think about the GPU architecture decisions that NVIDIA made early on, were there specific design choices that you think accidentally positioned you perfectly for the AI revolution, or was there genuine foresight in those decisions? Jensen Huang: I would say it was a combination. The fundamental bet we made was on programmable shading. In the early days of GPUs, everything was fixed function. You had a triangle rasterizer, a texture mapper, and that was it. We made the decision to make the GPU fully programmable, and that was a controversial decision at the time because it added cost and complexity. But it meant that researchers could repurpose our hardware for things we never imagined. CUDA was the bridge. When we launched CUDA in 2006, people thought we were crazy. Why would a graphics company create a general purpose programming model? But that single decision is what allowed the deep learning community to adopt our GPUs for training neural networks. Lex Fridman: Speaking of CUDA, do you ever worry about the competitive moat around that ecosystem? There are efforts to create alternatives, open standards that could potentially allow other hardware to compete more effectively. How do you think about defending that position? Jensen Huang: Competition is healthy and I welcome it. Our moat is not just CUDA as a language. It is the entire ecosystem. It is the libraries, the frameworks, the tools, the developer community, the decades of optimization. When a researcher writes code using cuDNN or TensorRT, they are building on top of millions of hours of engineering work. You cannot replicate that overnight. That said, we never take our position for granted. We invest billions of dollars every year in research and development because we know that complacency is the enemy of innovation. Lex Fridman: Let us talk about the future for a moment. There is a lot of debate about artificial general intelligence and when or if it will arrive. Some people think we are just a few years away, others think it is decades or more. Where do you fall on that spectrum? Jensen Huang: I think the answer depends on how you define AGI. If you mean a system that can pass any test a human can pass, I think we are closer than most people realize. Large language models already demonstrate remarkable reasoning capabilities. They can write code, analyze data, engage in complex discussions. The missing pieces are things like persistent memory, better planning, and genuine understanding versus pattern matching. I believe we will see systems within the next five to ten years that blur the line between narrow and general intelligence in ways that will be genuinely transformative for society.
